body.answer: Top-ranked HVAC companies have a median of 519 Google reviews.
body.html: Our own scrape of Google Business Profiles found that the HVAC contractors sitting on page 1 of Google Maps in major Texas metros have a median of 519 reviews (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026). That is the number to beat. It is also the highest review bar of any trade we measured, by a wide margin.

body.h2: Why 519 is so much higher than other trades
body.answer: HVAC has the steepest review bar of any trade we scraped.
body.html:
Here is the full review bar from our scrape, ranked highest to lowest (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026): HVAC 519, Plumbing 337, Roofing 144, Painting 109, Electrical 64.

HVAC needs more than three times the reviews that painters need, and more than eight times what electricians need. Why? Lots of HVAC companies fight for the same towns, and homeowners call a lot when the AC dies in a Texas summer. More jobs, more competitors, more reviews. The bar climbs.

This is the non-obvious part: the review bar is not a trophy, it is the price of entry. Hitting 519 does not put you on top. It gets you into the room with the contractors who already rank. Below it, you are mostly invisible on the map.

body.h2: Reviews are the thing buyers actually look at
body.answer: Almost everyone reads reviews before they pick a contractor.
body.html:
This bar matters because reviews are how people decide. 91% of people read local reviews, and most won't even consider a business under 4 stars (BrightLocal 2025). 81% rely on Google reviews specifically (CallRail 2026).

So two things have to be true. You need enough reviews to clear the bar and rank, and you need the stars high enough that people don't scroll past you. A pile of reviews with a 3.6 average works against you. Volume and rating both count.

body.h2: Responding to reviews is free leverage
body.answer: Replying to every review makes more buyers favor you.
body.html:
Most contractors get this wrong. 88% of people would use a business that responds to all its reviews (BrightLocal 2024). That is a free edge. It costs nothing but a few minutes, and most of your competitors aren't doing it.

Reply to the good ones with a thank-you. Reply to the bad ones calm and professional. People reading later see how you handle a problem, and that often matters more than the complaint itself.

body.answer: Ask every happy customer, every time, the same day.
body.html:
There is no trick. You get reviews by asking, and by asking consistently. The companies at 519 didn't get there by accident. They built a habit: finish the job, ask for the review while the customer is happy, send a direct link to their Google profile.

Do the math on your own pace. If a tech closes out a handful of jobs a day and even a fraction leave a review, you build steadily over months. The contractors ahead of you have been doing this for years. The only way to catch up is to start the habit now and never drop it.

body.h2: Reviews protect the leads you already pay for
body.answer: Reviews lift the return on every dollar you spend getting found.
body.html:
HVAC already has strong paid economics. On Google Local Services Ads, HVAC runs about a $51 cost per lead, 44% of those leads book, an average ticket around $2,110, and a 9.55x return on ad spend (SearchLight 2026).

But ads only put you in front of people. Reviews are what make them pick you. When someone sees your ad or your map listing and then sees 500-plus reviews at a strong rating, they trust you before they call. Your reviews and your ad spend work together. Weak reviews waste the clicks you paid for.

faq.q: How many Google reviews does an HVAC company need to rank?
faq.a: Our scrape found the median HVAC contractor ranking on page 1 of Google Maps in major Texas metros has 519 reviews (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026). That is the bar to clear to compete, not to guarantee the top spot.

faq.q: Why do HVAC companies need more reviews than other trades?
faq.a: HVAC has the highest bar we measured: 519 versus Plumbing 337, Roofing 144, Painting 109, and Electrical 64 (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026). More competitors and more frequent calls in a market push the review count higher.

faq.q: Do star ratings matter as much as review count?
faq.a: Yes. 91% of people read local reviews and most won't consider a business under 4 stars (BrightLocal 2025). You need both the volume to rank and a strong rating so buyers don't scroll past you.

title: How Many Google Reviews an HVAC Company Needs to Rank (We Scraped It: 519)
h1: How many Google reviews does an HVAC company need to rank? We scraped it.
meta: We scraped Google profiles across major TX metros: top-ranked HVAC contractors have a median of 519 reviews (Booked Job, 2026). What it means and how to get there.
short: We scraped Google Business Profiles across major Texas metros. The HVAC contractors ranking on page 1 of Google Maps have a median of 519 reviews (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026). That's the highest bar of any trade we measured — and it's the price of entry to compete, not a guarantee of the top spot.
statLabel: Median Google reviews of page-1 HVAC contractors in major TX metros (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026)

_verdict.notes:
Every numeric/dollar claim in the article is supported by the approved list with correct source attribution. Verified claim by claim:

GBP REVIEW BAR (Booked Job GBP scrape, 2026): HVAC 519, Plumbing 337, Roofing 144, Painting 109, Electrical 64 — all match approved exactly, cited correctly throughout (statBig, body, FAQ). The derived comparisons are arithmetically sound and not presented as separate stats: "more than three times the reviews that painters need" (519/109 = 4.76x, so "more than three times" is true/conservative), "more than eight times what electricians need" (519/64 = 8.1x, true). Good — these are framed as ratios of approved numbers, not invented figures.

REVIEWS/RATINGS: 91% read local reviews + won't consider under 4 stars (BrightLocal 2025) — matches approved. 81% rely on Google reviews (CallRail 2026) — matches. 88% would use a business that responds to all its reviews (BrightLocal 2024) — matches.

LSA HVAC: ~$51 CPL, 44% book, ~$2,110 ticket, 9.55x ROAS (SearchLight 2026) — matches approved exactly, attributed to "SearchLight 2026" correctly. Note: approved list shows "$2,110 ticket" for HVAC LSA and article says "average ticket around $2,110" — correct.

No invented illustrative dollar amounts. The "How to actually get to 519" section deliberately avoids fabricating a per-day review count ("a tech closes out a handful of jobs a day and even a fraction leave a review") — kept vague, no invented numbers. Good compliance.

The "500-plus reviews" phrasing in the body is a loose restatement of the approved 519 figure, not a new stat — acceptable.

TONE: Plain, direct, trade-audience appropriate. No hype, no fabricated urgency. The "price of entry, not a trophy" framing is editorial opinion, not a stat claim. Tone is fine.
